coredata.dc:description: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) are formed by combining lignite, which primarily forms carbon cores, with polyvinylpyrrolidone, citric acid and tryptophan, which primarily produce short polymer chains and surface functional groups. These CPDs display a unique core–shell structure, excellent optical stability, superior fluorescence intensity, high quantum yield (QY) and outstanding biocompatibility allowing them to be well suited for application as fluorescent sensors. CPDs can be employed both as smart sensing systems for visual detection and absorption of Cu2+ and S2− through hydrogel sensors and as fluorescent inks for information security and anti-counterfeiting. More importantly, the paper-based platform and smartphone assistance enable visual real-time on-site quantification of Cu2+ and S2−, which offers an effective tool for fast and accurate Cu2+ and S2− identification and information encryption with high selectivity and sensitivity. In addition, with its good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, the CPDs have been successfully utilized for imaging Cu2+ and S2− in MG-63 cells, thus making a positive contribution to the analysis and imaging of the effects of Cu2+ and S2− in relevant biological processes and promoting better diagnosis and treatment of diseases.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the route to synthesize CPDs.

Morphology, structural and functional group characterization of CPDs: (a) TEM image, particle size distribution and HRTEM image (insets). (b) XRD pattern. (c) Raman spectra. (d) FT-IR spectra. (e) XPS full spectra and (f-h) split peak fitting of C 1s, N1s and O1s. (i) TGA and DTG analysis.

Characterization of optical properties of CPDs: (a) UV–Vis, best excitation and emission fluorescence spectra. (b) Emission spectra at different wavelengths of excitation. (c) Fluorescence decay curves. (d-f) Fluorescence stability at different storage times, 0–1.0 mol·L−1 concentration of NaCl solution and different UV lamp irradiation times, respectively.

Characterization of the sensing performance of CPDs: (a) Selectivity (red column) and competitive detection (green column) for Cu2+. (b) pH investigation of the CPDs (1 mg·L−1) in and without the presence of 200 μmol·L−1 of Cu2+ in aqueous solution with different pH conditions. (c) Fluorescence spectra of CPDs with the addition of different concentrations of Cu2+ from 0.01 to 500 μmol·L−1 (inset: Linear relationship between CPDs and different concentrations of Cu2+). (d) Selectivity (green column) and competitive detection (red column). (e) pH investigation of CPDs+Cu2+ in and without the presence of 200 μmol·L−1 of S2− in aqueous solution with different pH conditions. (f) Fluorescence spectra of CPDs+Cu2+ at the addition of different concentrations of S2− from 0.01 to 500 μmol·L−1 (inset: Linear relationship between CPDs+Cu2+ and different concentrations of S2−).

Characterization of the sensing mechanism of CPDs for Cu2+ and S2−: (a) Fluorescence decay curves. (b) Zeta potential, (c) UV–Vis and (d) FT-IR spectra of CPDs, CPDs+Cu2+ and CPDs+Cu2++S2−, respectively. (e) XPS full spectrum of CPDs+Cu2+ and (f-h) split peak fits of N1s, O1s and Cu2p, respectively. (i) High-resolution S2p spectrum of CPDs+Cu2++S2−.
Schematic illustration of the mechanism of Cu2+ and S2− detection by CPDs.

Smart sensing applications of CPDs: paper-based sensors detect Cu2+ (a) and (b) S2−. (c) Diagram illustrating the process of detecting Cu2+ and S2− by smart sensing systems. Linear relationship between H/V values and (d) Cu2+ concentration and (e) S2− concentration, respectively. (f) Relationship between storage time of paper-based sensors and H/V values.
Fluorescent ink applications for CPDs: message encryption, (daylight: a1-b1, UV light: a2-b4); anti-counterfeit printing (daylight: c1-f1, UV light: c2-f4).

(a) Schematic illustration of the recognition mechanism of Cu2+ and S2− by the composite sensor composed of CPDs and hydrogel. (b) From left to right, hydrogel alone, CPDs+hydrogel, CPDs+hydrogel+Cu2+, CPDs+Cu2++hydrogel+S2−. (c) Images of Cu2+ in the absorption solution of CPDs-hydrogel with time. (d) Images of S2− in the absorption solution of CPDs+Cu2++hydrogel with time.

Live cells sensing applications of CPDs: series 1: images of MG-63 cells; series 2: images of cells incubated with CPDs (1 mg∙L−1) at 37 °C for 30 min; series 3: images of the above cells incubated with 200 μmol·L−1 of Cu2+ at 37 °C for 30 min; series 4: images of the cells of series 3 incubated with 200 μmol·L−1 of S2− at 37 °C for further 30 min. (a) Bright-field transmission images of cells. (b) Dark-field transmission images of cells. (c) Overlay images of (a) and (b).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) are formed by combining lignite, which primarily forms carbon cores, with polyvinylpyrrolidone, citric acid and tryptophan, which primarily produce short polymer chains and surface functional groups. These CPDs display a unique core–shell structure, excellent optical stability, superior fluorescence intensity, high quantum yield (QY) and outstanding biocompatibility allowing them to be well suited for application as fluorescent sensors. CPDs can be employed both as smart sensing systems for visual detection and absorption of Cu2+ and S2− through hydrogel sensors and as fluorescent inks for information security and anti-counterfeiting. More importantly, the paper-based platform and smartphone assistance enable visual real-time on-site quantification of Cu2+ and S2−, which offers an effective tool for fast and accurate Cu2+ and S2− identification and information encryption with high selectivity and sensitivity. In addition, with its good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, the CPDs have been successfully utilized for imaging Cu2+ and S2− in MG-63 cells, thus making a positive contribution to the analysis and imaging of the effects of Cu2+ and S2− in relevant biological processes and promoting better diagnosis and treatment of diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most abundant elements with multiple electron hybridization orbital properties, carbon is not only closely related to humans but also indispensable in nature [1–4]. Employing it as the primary structure of nanomaterials can manifest diverse structural variations and modifications, successfully allowing materials to achieve structural extension and derivation from zero to multi-dimensional [5–8]. Among the emerging zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, CPDs which was first put forward by Yang et al. [9] and belongs to the high-profiled material of carbon dots (CDs), displays carbonized polymer hybrid special “core–shell” nanostructures which differ both from typical CDs materials such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as well as polymer dots [10–14]. CPDs can be considered as a new material in transition in which the sp2/sp3 carbon cores are usually constituted by highly dehydrated crosslinking polymer frames or slight graphitization in combination with the abundant functional groups and polymer chains to formulate the outer shells undergoing dehydration, polymerization and carbonization processes [15–19].

Encouragingly, the unique carbon/polymer core–shell structural construction and special photoluminescence mechanism ensure the structure-performance relationship of CPDs highly controllable by regulating the carbon core and functional group rich polymer chains [20–22]. Accordingly, the CPDs presents superior optical properties, excellent water solubility, remarkable stability and compatibility, good biocompatibility and environmental friendliness [23–25]. As a result, CPDs has showed enormous wide application potential in lots of aspects especially for fluorescent sensing, in vivo imaging, drug or protein delivery as well as disease diagnosis and treatments [26–30]. Especially, it is advisable and appropriate to design CPDs as excellent fluorescent sensors toward multitudinous interesting targets [31–33].

Among which, copper ion (Cu2+), as one of the most important elements affecting human health, can be taken up by water or the food chain and is not only involved in a variety of essential physiological processes but has been demonstrated to have the ability to induce accelerated protein misfolding and aggregation forming amyloid fibrils, which is highly correlated with the basic principle of Parkinson’s (PD) pathogenesis [1,34–37]. More intriguingly, hydrogen sulfide (H2S), which can be converted to each other with sulfur anion (S2−) through bisulfates (HS−), has been increasingly evidenced as potentially valuable in the treatment of PD, owing to the fact that it not only reverses neuronal damage in animal models of PD, thereby alleviating the accompanying disease, but also reduces oxidative stress by removing abnormal concentrations of reactive oxygen species (ROS) from mitochondria, and thereby achieving protective effect [38–40]. In addition, S2− can be emitted from biological metabolism and industrial production in concentrations that would be life-threatening if they exceeded the maximum acceptable range for humans [41–43]. Thus, the development of effective tools with dual functionality for the assay of Cu2+ and S2− has far-reaching implications for environmental, biological and health concerns in combination with the promoting for better understanding of the pathogenesis and therapeutic mechanisms of PD.

Compared with traditional methods such as atomic absorption spectroscopy [44], chromatography [45] and electrochemistry [46,47] which are generally limited by the need for complex instrumentation, cumbersome sample preparation and high costs, the fluorescent probe based on CPDs not only enjoys the advantages of low cost, ease of operation and excellent reproducibility, but also avoids the high cytotoxicity, poor water solubility and low photostability which usually hinder the application the fluorescent sensors based on organic small molecule dyes and semiconductor quantum dots in biosensing and imaging [48–50]. In the CPDs based sensor, the electron rich polymer chains with abundant surface functional groups display convincingly coordinating attraction toward the electron deficient Cu2+ which subsequently quenches the fluorescent emission of the carbon core [51]. Whereas, the Cu2+ can be easily separated from the coordination chains with addition of S2− and then recover the fluorescence, thus provide the possibility for successively detection of Cu2+ and S2− with the CPDs based fluorescent sensor [52].

Here, we have successfully synthesized attractive CPDs with superior optical properties, favorable water solubility, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and many other advantages by simple green hydrothermal method using lignite, polyvinylpyrrolidone, citric acid and tryptophan as raw materials (Fig. 1 ). In the construction of CPDs, lignite facilitates the formation of sp2 carbon cores. In addition, the various reactions that occur between the raw materials lead to cross-linking and the formation of low molecular weight polymers, which are then further carbonized into an inner core, while the outer shell remains in a state of short polymer chains with an abundance of functional groups on the surface.

The rich surface functional groups and favorable properties guarantee the CPDs as promising fluorescent sensor for responding of Cu2+ and S2− through fluorescence “on-off-on” process. Furthermore, the bright blue fluorescence, good biocompatibility and excellently water solubility allow them with great advantages in practical applications as smart sensing system. In this study, both the paper and hydrogel based sensors have been successfully applied as solid sensors for low-level Cu2+ and S2− identification. Specifically, the paper-based sensor system combined with smartphone can accurately quantify the subject by programmatically recognizing changes in fluorescence color which is beneficial to biological applications where precise concentrations are required. The cost-effective, convenient and highly sensitive make the CPDs based smart sensing system promising for a wide range of sensing applications which laying the foundation for the development of fluorescent sensors and help better understandings of the ions levels in environments and living organisms.
Lignite was purchased from Chuangye Energy Technology Co., (Shaanxi, China). Polyvinylpyrrolidone, citric acid and tryptophan were purchased from Alfa Aesar Chemical Co., (Shanghai, China).

First, 0.5 g of lignite was dispersed in 150 mL of distilled water and 0.5 g of polyvinylpyrrolidone, 0.5 g of citric acid and 0.5 g of tryptophan were added to the water and stirred thoroughly. They were then uniformly transferred to three 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclaves and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction had been completed and cooled to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove large impurities. Next, the unreacted material was filtered through 0.2 μm microporous membrane and the liquid was dialyzed through 5000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h for the pure CPDs, which were then vacuum freeze dried to obtain pale yellow powder solid and stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

Lignite has more advantageous as precursors of CPDs, facilitating the formation of sp2 carbon nuclei due to its abundant nanoscale graphite crystals connected by aliphatic amorphous carbon. Besides, a variety of reactions that occur between lignite with polyvinylpyrrolidone, citric acid and tryptophan result in the cross-link to form low molecular weight polymers, which are then further carbonized into the inner core, whereas the outer shell remains in the state of short polymer chains with abundant surface functional groups of amino, hydroxy and carboxyl groups derived from citric acid and tryptophan, resulting in the formation of the new carbon nanomaterial CPDs.

The structural properties and elemental composition of CPDs were characterized by the following instruments. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) was performed by JEM–1400 Flash analyzer. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was investigated from Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer. The Raman spectra was measured Thermo Fisher DXR2 spectroscopy. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was acquired using Bruker Tensor 27 spectrometer. The X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was performed by PHI-5000VersaProbeIII spectrophotometer. The Zeta potential data were interpreted by a JS94H microelectrophoresis instrument. The optical properties of CPDs were explained by the characterization results of such instrumentation. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were revealed through Hitachi U–2900 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectroscopy was presented by Horiba FluoroMax 4 fluorescence spectrometer. The fluorescence lifetime was calculated from Edinburgh FLS920 steady/transient fluorescence spectrometer. The information under Xenon lamp irradiation was achieved on CEL-HXF300-T3 xenon lamp source. The application of CPDs is implemented by the following tools. Paper strip sensors were produced by a Canon TS3480 printer. Cytotoxicity test was collected on ELx800 Absorbance Reader. Cell imaging was carried on Olympus FV1000 confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cu2+ and S2−
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of Cu2+ by CPDs: (1). Selective: 0.5 mL of 1 mg·L−1 solution of CPDs was mixed separately with 0.5 mL of 50 mmol·L−1 solutions of various ions (Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, Sn4+, O2 ·−, H2O2, NO, ONOO−, ClO−, SO3 2−, AA, GSH, Hcy, Cys, Cu2+), then set to 10 mL with phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and shaken well. The fluorescence spectra of liquids at room temperature were recorded at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. (2). Competitive: 0.5 mL of 1 mg·L−1 solution of CPDs was mixed with 0.5 mL of 50 mmol·L−1 solution of Cu2+, followed by the addition of 5 times the concentration solutions of other ions to it respectively, then fixed to 10 mL with PBS and shaken well, and the fluorescence data of the mixtures were recorded. (3). Linearity and detection limit of detection (LOD): 0.5 mL of 1 mg·L−1 solution of CPDs was mixed with 0.5 mL of different concentrations of 0.01–500 μmol·L−1 Cu2+ solutions respectively, and then adjusted to 10 mL with PBS and shaken well, finally the fluorescence spectra of the above reactants were recorded.

Detection of S2− by CPDs+Cu2+: (1). Selectivity: 0.5 mL of the above mixture of CPDs solution and Cu2+ solution was used with 0.5 mL of 50 mmol·L−1 of various ions (AMP, ADP, ATP, O2 ·−, H2O2, NO, ONOO−, ClO−, PO4 3−, CO3 2−, OAc−, C2O4 2−, SO3 2−, DA, AA, GSH, Hcy, Cys, S2−) were mixed and then set to 10 mL with PBS and shaken well. The fluorescence spectra of liquids were recorded at room temperature at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. (2). Competitive: 0.5 mL of the mixture of CPDs solution (1 mg·L−1) and Cu2+ solution (200 μmol·L−1) was then mixed with 0.5 mL of 50 mmol·L−1 solution of S2−, followed by 5 times the concentration of the other ions to it respectively, then fixed to 10 mL with PBS and shaken well, and the fluorescence data of the mixtures were recorded. (3). Linearity and LOD: 0.5 mL of the above mixture of CPDs solution (1 mg·L−1) and Cu2+ solution (200 μmol·L−1) was mixed with 0.5 mL different concentrations of 0.01–500 μmol·L−1 S2− solutions respectively, and then adjusted to 10 mL with PBS and shaken well, finally the fluorescence spectra of the above reactants were recorded.

CPDs-based solid sensors are prepared using filter paper (1.5 × 1.5 cm) and solution of CPDs. Typically, the filter papers were completely immersed in a solution of CPDs at the concentration of 1 mg·L−1, removed after 10 min and allowed to air dry. Subsequently, 1 mL of different concentrations (0.01–500 μmol·L−1) of Cu2+ solutions were applied to these paper-based sensors in drops, left to dry at room temperature for 10 min, and then photographed with smartphone under UV light. Likewise, 30 μL of different concentrations (0.01–500 μmol·L−1) of S2− solutions was added dropwise to the paper-based sensors containing CPDs+Cu2+ solution and subjected to similar treatment as above.

To 0.1 g of CPDs solids 10 mL of distilled water was added and stirred. Then, 2.5 g of monomer (acrylamide) and 0.05 g of initiator (ammonium persulphate) were added to the above solution and sonicated for 3–5 min in ice water bath until the materials was completely dissolved. The mixture is poured into the molds and then sonicated for 1–2 min to remove air bubbles. Finally, the reaction was carried out in oven at 65 °C for 1.5 h in order to produce fluorescent hydrogels based on CPDs.

The adsorption properties of CPDs+hydrogel were determined by adding 0.1 g of dry hydrogels containing CPDs to 100 mL of 0.1 g∙L−1 Cu2+ solution.
Q e
=
V (
C 0
-
C e
)
m
Qe is the amount of Cu2+ adsorbed by the CPDs+hydrogel when adsorption reaches equilibrium, C0 stands the concentration of Cu2+ in the aqueous solution before adsorption, Ce matches the concentration of Cu2+ in the aqueous solution at adsorption equilibrium, V refers to the volume of the solution, and m serves the mass of CPDs+hydrogel. Similarly, tests were carried out for the adsorption of S2− in aqueous solutions by CPDs+Cu2+-hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and S2− in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Both lake and tap water samples were obtained from Northwestern University and filtered through filtration membranes before use. The concentration of Cu2+ in the samples was determined based on the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CPDs and the concentration of Cu2+, and then the recovery was obtained. The determination of S2− recovery was similar to the above procedure.

Cytotoxicity of CPDs: As for the methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay, MG-63 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well, in which medium was added to each well and incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. The cells were then incubated with different concentrations (0–1000 mg·mL−1) of CPDs solution for 24 h at the same way. The medium was then aspirated and the cells were washed three times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) before being placed on shaker for 10 min to measure their absorbance.

Cell imaging of CPDs: Human Osteosarcoma MG-63 cells were washed with PBS and imaged after incubation in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. The cells were then washed three times with PBS and imaged after adding 1 mg·L−1 of CPDs to the medium and incubating for 30 min under the same conditions. Next, 200 mmol·L−1 Cu2+ and S2− solutions were added to the above cells in sequence and fluorescence imaging was performed separately.

In the synthesis of CPDs, four substances namely lignite, polyvinylpyrrolidone, citric acid and tryptophan are indispensable as raw materials. Specifically, lignite is the preferred member of the carbon nucleus because it is rich in aliphatic aromatic rings and is cheap and easy to obtain. Further, the carbonyl groups on lignite and polyvinylpyrrolidone can react with the Schiff base of the active amino group on tryptophan. The carboxyl groups on lignite and citric acid can also engage in dehydration condensation reaction with the amino group on tryptophan, and plenty low molecular weight polymer chains are obtained by the interaction of these substances. After polymerization and hypo-carbonization of these substances, CPDs are produced with a quasi-crystalline carbon core and a highly cross-linked polymer chain structure with a broad range of amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface (Fig. 1). To better evaluate the morphology, structural and chemical composition of CPDs influencing on the optical properties, it was characterized by TEM, XRD, Raman, FT-IR, XPS and other techniques. The TEM image in Fig. 2 a shows the CPDs is dispersed uniformly and presented in quasi-spherical morphology with an average particle size calculated to be 3.91 ± 0.79 nm (inset of Fig. 2a). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) exhibits that the carbon core of the CPDs has distinct lattice stripes of 0.21 nm which resembles the 100 facets of graphite (inset of Fig. 2a) [28]. The XRD spectrum was taken to further analyze the crystallinity of CPDs and a sharp diffraction peak near the center at 2θ = 22° belongs to the (1 0 0) crystalline plane of graphite which indirectly supports the results obtained in the HRTEM image (Fig. 2b) [53]. The Raman spectroscopy demonstrates that the D band (carbon defects) of CPDs is located at 1332 cm−1 and the G band (degree of carbonization) at 1562 cm−1, the peak intensity ratio ID/IG = 0.94 implies that the carbon nuclei in CPDs tend to be ordered sp2 carbon which indicates that lignite contributes to the formation of carbon nuclei (Fig. 2c) [22].

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to explore the surface functional groups of CPDs, as shown in the Fig. 2d the peak at 3427 cm−1 is caused by stretching vibrations of N-H/O-H, while the C-H is observed at 2920 cm−1, there is a clear peak at 1716 cm−1 assigned to CO, the peaks of N-H and CO bonds indicate that the precursor undergoes dehydration condensation to form an amide structure, a strong peak at 1620 cm−1 is associated with CC/CN bond which confirms that CPDs has conjugated aromatic ring structure and occurs Schiff base reaction, the peak at 1424 cm−1 implies the presence of CN, and there is also a peak at 1213 cm−1 which belongs to CO bond [54]. Thus, CPDs contains such as –NH2, –OH and –COOH groups on the surface and the abundant presence of these functional groups confirms the polymer/carbon hybrid structure and ensures its remarkable fluorescence property, water solubility and target recognition ability. Additionally, the Zeta potential of CPDs of −12.335 mV reveals a high level of anionic groups (e.g. –OH and –COOH) on the surface (Fig. 5b) [55].

To further validate the above characterization results, XPS spectra were performed as shown in Fig. 2e, the CPDs contains the following three elements C, N and O at 71.75%, 10.27% and 17.98% respectively. In addition, the high-resolution spectrum of C1s can be subdivided into four peaks at 284.18 eV (CC), 284.80 eV (CC), 285.49 eV (CN/CO) and 287.20 eV (CN/CO), respectively (Fig. 2f) [32]. The fine spectra of the N1s illustrates in Fig. 2g was fitted to the peaks at 397.65 eV, 398.34 eV and 399.41 eV, which are affiliated with CN, CN and NH2, respectively. Meanwhile, the spectra of O1s at 530.02 eV and 531.60 eV represent the peaks at CO and CO, respectively (Fig. 2h). Overall, the results of the XPS spectral analysis remain in high agreement with the FT-IR spectra, further supporting the characteristics of the CPDs.

In the 1H NMR spectra of CPDs, the chemical shift at 1–4 ppm is attributed to the alkyl chain, the presence of the amide bond is evidenced by a peak located at 10–12 ppm chemical shift, while the 6–8 ppm range belongs to hydrogen on the aromatic ring (Fig. S1) [9]. Subsequently, CPDs was further identified by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) as featuring unique core (carbon)/shell (polymer chain) structures [20,56,57]. As observed in the Fig. 2i, the composition of the weight loss prior to 250 °C is most likely to be water of crystallization. The second degradation occurs in the range of approximately 350–450 °C, which is mainly believed to be the gradual cleavage of the polymer chains. When the temperature exceeds 450 °C, the active material in the region where the core coexists with the polymer chains is disintegrated. Eventually, after reaching 650 °C, the carbon core of the CPDs has residual weight of about 5% and remains stable.

Following the definition of the structural characteristics and chemical composition of CPDs [58], it is worthwhile to examine the photoluminescence properties thoroughly with help of UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The aqueous solution of CPDs displays bright blue fluorescence when irradiated by UV lamp at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 3 a). The UV–Vis spectrum is depicted in Fig. 3a, the broad absorption band at 278 nm corresponds mainly to the π-π* transition of CC in the sp2 carbon domain of the aromatic chromophore structure [59], while the peak near 350 nm is responsible for the n-π* transitions of the surface function groups of CO and CN [60]. Further, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of CPDs are 350 nm and 441 nm, respectively. The wavelength-independent fluorescence property of CPDs is explained in Fig. 3b, by adjusting the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 400 nm, the emission wavelength of CPDs is subsequently red-shifted by only nearly 20 nm, which may be owing to the more regular and homogeneous structure of polymer. In addition, using the fluorescence QY (54%) of 0.1 mol·L−1 H2SO4 as a reference, the QY of CPDs was calculated to be 16.70%, which is a satisfactory result compared to that reported previously in the literature [61]. The fluorescence decay curve of CPDs was fitted with an average lifetime of 11.34 ns consisting mainly of the fast component τ1 = 1.22 ns (2.30%) and the slower component τ2 = 11.58 ns (97.70%), indicating that the radiative lifetime of CPDs is dominated by the functional groups on the surface (Fig. 3c) [48].

Outstanding probes need to maintain good fluorescence stability across a wide range of situations [31]. As evident in the Fig. 3d, the fluorescence intensity of CPDs is virtually constant over a period of up to 60 days, which is ideal for tracking targets in vivo real-time over long periods of time. Additionally, in order to investigate the ability of CPDs to adapt in complex and variable environments within organisms, it was found that fluorescence loss was no more than 5% of its original value in 0.1–1.0 mol·L−1 NaCl solutions and in solutions in the range of pH = 1.7–10, signifying that it is extremely resistant to ionic interference (Fig. 3e) and is capable of monitoring cells with abnormal pH (Fig. S2). More importantly, the CPDs maintains excellent fluorescence emission without interference for up to 120 min when irradiated with either 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 3f) or Xenon lamp of 1500 mW·cm−2 excitation wavelength (Fig. S3). One of the external confounding factors, temperature could not be ignored, when aqueous solutions of CPDs were placed below room temperature (0–25 °C), it was surprising to find that their fluorescence intensity was not disturbed (Fig. S4); even more remarkable was the fact that CPDs maintained excellent fluorescence even at temperatures between 25 and 100 °C (Fig. S5). The above experiments highlight the amazing ability of CPDs for fluorescence imaging of targets in vivo and independent of external influences.

It is necessary for CPDs to has a precise and rapid response towards Cu2+ and S2− for better assessment of the relationship between these two substances and human health. As seen in Fig. 4 a, when a total of 30 substances (blank, Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, Sn4+, O2 ·−, H2O2, NO, ONOO−, ClO−, SO3 2−, AA, GSH, Hcy, Cys, S2−, Cu2+) of solutions were added to the aqueous solution of CPDs, it was noticed that the fluorescence of CPDs was quenched in the presence of only the target Cu2+, meaning that CPDs has excellent selectivity for Cu2+ and the recognition process was completed in less than 1 min (Fig. S6). Moreover, the strong resistance of CPDs to interference confers the ability to be unaffected by other ions. To further verify the immunity of the CPDs, the mixture of CPDs and Cu2+ was placed in different pH environments, which still ensure a good response to Cu2+ over a wide range of pH = 5–13 (Fig. 4b). Finally, Fig. 4c presents the relationship between the concentration of Cu2+ and the fluorescence intensity of CPDs, with a linear response in the range of 0.1–200 μmol·L−1 (R2 = 0.99632) and the LOD of 0.059 μmol·L−1 (S/N = 3), which is not only a more excellent result compared with previous reports (Tables S1, S2, S3, S5), satisfying the national standard for the detection of Cu2+ in domestic drinking water (GB5749-2022), but also this has implications for the assessment of trace copper in cells.

After demonstrating the ability of CPDs to accurately detect Cu2+ associated with the development of PD and environment pollution. As evidenced in the Fig. S7, the hybrid probe of CPDs and Cu2+ can sense the import of S2− correlating with the serious diseases in less than 1 min. Similarly unaffected by interfering substances including blank, AMP, ADP, ATP, O2 ·−, H2O2, NO, ONOO−, ClO−, PO4 3−, CO3 2−, OAc−, C2O4 2−, SO3 2−, DA, AA, GSH, Hcy, Cys, S2− (Fig. 4d). Furthermore, the sensor is more suitable in the range of pH = 3.5–9, which is consistent with the atmosphere in most organisms (Fig. 4e). As expected, the fluorescence intensity of CPDs at 441 nm gradually increased as the concentration of S2− increased from 0.01 to 500 μmol·L−1 (Fig. 4f). Fig. 4f inset exemplifies that the composite sensor of CPDs+Cu2+ has a good linear response to S2− at concentrations in the range 0.1–200 μmol·L−1 (R2 = 0.9942) accompanied by LOD as low as 0.032 μmol·L−1 and remains a superior result compared with previous literature reporting S2− detection (Tables S1, S2, S4, S5) and meets the national standard for Sulphur content in drinking water (GB5749-2022). Given the ultra-high affinity of the above-prepared sensors for Cu2+ and S2−, their reversible sensing properties were also explored. The fluorescence of the mixture was measured after adding Cu2+ to the original CPDs solution, and then, the fluorescence intensity was also measured by adding S2− to the above solution. As shown in Fig. S8, the sensitivity of the CPDs remained above 85% of the original level after three repeated cycles, which is a strong indication that it can be cycled for the sequential detection of Cu2+ and S2−.

There is still demand for further research on how the fluorescence transition from “off” (CPDs+Cu2+) to “on” (CPDs+Cu2++S2−) is achieved by S2−. Firstly, the lifetime of CPDs+Cu2++S2− was calculated to be 10.03 ns by transient fluorescence spectroscopy, probably because the stronger Cu-S bond that would lead to loss of coordination and hence recovery of fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 5a) [53]. Further, it has Zeta potential of −8.578 mV, which is caused by the negatively charged S2− inhibiting the binding of Cu2+ to the functional groups (Fig. 5b). The UV spectrum is shown in the Fig. 5c, the disappearance of the peak represented by the Cu-O bond confirms the detachment of Cu2+ from the surface of CPDs and their subsequent binding to S2− [62], while the reappearance of the peak at 350 nm indirectly demonstrates the recovery of fluorescence from the CPDs. The FT-IR spectrum highlights that the transmittance of Cu-O is greatly reduced (Fig. 5d), as a result the most of the Cu2+ detaching from the surface of the CPDs and binding to the S2− [23], and the XPS spectrum of S2p in Fig. 5i is also consistent with the above view, which can be fitted to S-Cu at 168.50 eV and S-H at 170.80 eV, respectively. Consequently, due to the fact that CuS has a rather low solubility constant (Ksp = 6.3 × 10−36), we believe that the addition of S2− has a higher coordination ability with Cu2+, leading to the detachment of Cu2+ from the surface of CPDs and thus fluorescence recovery (Fig. 6) [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based smart sensing platform fluorescence detection of Cu2+ and S2−
ce:section.ce:para: For the intuitive and easy analysis of the analytes, the fluorescent paper-based sensor was prepared for the visual detection of Cu2+ and S2−. Fig. 7 a depicts a slow drop of a solution of various ions at a concentration of 50 mmol·L−1 onto paper strips (containing CPDs), which were then dried and found to fluoresce uniformly and brightly in blue, except for the strips spiked with the solution with Cu2+ whose fluorescence was quenched under 365 nm UV irradiation. With a view to checking the recognition of S2− by the paper-based sensor, strips of paper (prepared from a mixture of CPDs and Cu2+) that did not emit luminescence under 365 nm irradiation were air-dried after being with different ionic solutions at concentration of 50 mmol·L−1, it was revealed that only the drops with the solution containing S2− regained their fluorescence, while the others remained as they were (Fig. 7b). It is possible to clearly observe the obvious fluorescence changes with the naked eye by means of this simple filter paper sensor. However, for practical applications, especially in the biological field, there are strict requirements for quantitative assays.

Therefore, based on this, we proposed a smartphone-loaded sensing device for fluorescence color change, which not only meets the requirements for fast, convenient, on-site, real-time quantitative analysis, but also has the advantages of high precision, low detection limit, environmental protection, sustainability and easy preparation. First, the CPDs-based fluorescence paper strips were added with different concentrations (0.01–500 μmol·L−1) of Cu2+ in solution, and then they were dried and placed in the dark to reduce the interference of ambient light. Under the irradiation of the UV lamp with excitation wavelength of 365 nm, images were taken with smartphone and their HSV values were obtained by using the color recognition software-color picker to identify the color of the strips in turn, which is a more intuitive representation of the hue, vividness and the brightness of the color and facilitates color comparison (Fig. 7c, Table S6). The Fig. 7d depicts that the H/V values of the solution and filter paper have a good linear relationship with the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 1–500 μmol·L−1 (y = 0.68744 + 0.00217x, R2 = 0.99923) and accompanied by LOD of 0.059 μmol·L−1, which is very satisfactory result and sufficient for the extraction of Cu2+ in daily life and environment. As well, fluorescent paper strips based on CPDs+Cu2+ were applied for the sensing of S2−, and it was determined that the H/V value of the paper sensor was linear in the range of 1–50 μmol·L−1 (y = 1.54654 – 0.00842x, R2 = 0.99524) with LOD of 0.033 μmol·L−1 (Fig. 7e). In addition, the color change of the paper-based sensor can be roughly judged by visual observation to assist in detecting the concentration of the substance, while the exact concentration needs to be quickly and accurately analyzed by smart sensing system. As shown in the Table S7, when different concentrations (1 μM (64 μg/L), 10 μM (640 μg/L), 50 μM (3200 μg/L)) of Cu2+ are added to the paper-based sensor, the concentration values obtained by the smartphone through analysis are essentially consistent with those obtained by inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometry (ICP), which is a more sensible choice for future environmental monitoring. More remarkably, the fluorescence intensity of the paper-based sensor containing CPDs was tracked for up to one month, concluding that it has good storage stability (Fig. 7f). The survey results confirm that the smartphone-assisted paper-based fluorescence sensing platform continues to perform well for rapid, accurate measurements of target Cu2+ and S2−.

Additionally, CPDs can shine as fluorescent ink in fields such as information encryption and security defense. As evident in the Fig. 8 a, b, the word “Carbon” is written on paper with a “fluorescent pen” containing solution of CPDs, and then a sentence was written on the same paper with a regular pen. In daylight, only the word written with the regular pen was visible. However, under UV light at 365 nm, the word written with the “fluorescent pen” was clearly visible. More meaningfully, this fluorescence was masked when Cu2+ are added to the solution while the introduction of S2− restores the original fluorescence. There are also Fig. 8 c-f are based on the same principle being printed as 2D code, bar code, light bulb and panda patterns, which all have the selectivity to emit light for displaying graphics and also interrupt fluorescence to hide information under conditions that are specifically required (Fig. S17). Therefore, this fluorescent ink combined with CPDs is suitable for confidential information and counterfeit pattern printing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogel fluorescence detection of Cu2+ and S2−
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to paper-based sensors prepared from CPDs, CPDs can be encapsulated in the hydrogels with unique three-dimensional structures to form the composite CPDs-hydrogel as sensor for bioassay applications (Fig. 9 a). In this part of the study, hydrogels synthesized with acrylamide and ammonium persulphate were colorless under UV light, while the CPDs-hydrogel had bright blue fluorescence under the same conditions (Fig. S18). To demonstrate the ability of CPDs-hydrogel to tracer Cu2+ and S2−, CPDs+hydrogel immersed in aqueous solution of Cu2+ were found to has greatly reduced fluorescence, surprisingly, with the addition of S2−, the CPDs+hydrogel quenched by Cu2+ regained fluorescence (Fig. 9b). Fig. 9c reveals the adsorption data of Cu2+ by CPDs+hydrogel, which almost reached the adsorption equilibrium at 90 min and the final adsorption capacity reached 77.874 mg·g−1, which means that the combination of CPDs and hydrogel has an extremely high level of adsorption capacity as an effective adsorbent for the removal of Cu2+. On the other hand, CPDs+Cu2++hydrogel can absorb S2− in solution with adsorption yield of about 26.519 mg·g−1 at 120 min (Fig. 9d). The presumed mechanism of the fluorescence reaction and hydrogel adsorption is approximately as follows: when the hydrogel containing CPDs is placed into an aqueous solution containing Cu2+, the accumulation and adsorption of Cu2+ is facilitated by the unique structure of the hydrogel and the abundance of functional groups on its surface. As a result, Cu2+ in solution can be rapidly adsorbed into the hydrogel. At the same time, the CPDs wrapped in these three-dimensional network structures act as a fluorescence source and quickly respond to Cu ions. However, when the hydrogel containing CPDs and Cu2+ was placed in an aqueous solution containing S2−, due to the strong binding of Cu2+ to S2−, S2− rapidly entered the network structure of the hydrogel and complexed with Cu2+, thus allowing Cu2+ to separate from the CPDs and restore fluorescence. There is a potential for CPDs-hydrogel composites to be developed as a biocompatible and environmentally friendly strategy to visually detect levels of Cu2+ and S2− in the biomedical field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of Cu2+ and S2− in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: In the practical application of CPDs, Cu2+ and S2− not only have vital impact in living organisms, but they are also present in the environment closely related to people’s lives and in crucial industrial production. Hence, the ability of the probe to sense Cu2+ and S2− in real water samples (lake water, tap water) requires evaluation. As presented in the Table S8, S9, the recoveries of 88.00–106.67% and 92.50–114.00% after adding different concentrations of Cu2+ and S2− to the water samples, respectively, fully confirm that CPDs as probe still has the capability to accurately assess ion levels in complex real-world environments, and also indicate that CPDs can not only be used as biological probe to assay ions in vivo but also play an active role in social production and life.

For extending the biological applications of CPDs, it is urgent to evaluate the levels of Cu2+ and S2− in vivo. Moreover, in order to explore the association of these ions with PD pathogenesis, labeling of Cu2+ and S2− in living cells should be taken into account in advance, and therefore CPDs need to be examined for MTT testing and cellular imaging [63]. We chose human osteosarcoma MG-63 cells as a representative not only because such cancer cells are generally highly biocompatible, tolerant and active, but also that Cu2+ and S2− are inextricably linked to cancer. In the MTT test Human Osteosarcoma MG-63 cells incubated with CPDs in the concentration range of 0–1000 mg·mL−1 have viability of up to 90% or more, which means that CPDs has very low cytotoxicity and is well adapted for cellular imaging (Fig. S19, Table S10). After incubating the cells for four hours with the sequential addition of CPDs, Cu2+ and S2− (Fig. S20), we observed that cell survival remained at high levels, demonstrating that the addition of these substances essentially unaffected biological activity.

Encouragingly, as seen in the Fig. 10 , the cells exhibited bright blue fluorescence after addition of the probe and co-incubation at 37 °C for 30 min. However, the fluorescence was completely quenched by the addition of 200 μmol·L−1 of Cu2+ to the cells after incubation. The cells then showed strong fluorescence after incubation with the addition of 200 μmol·L−1 of S2−. Besides, we added the control A549 human non-small cell lung cancer cell line and Hela human cervical cancer cell line for comparison experiments shown in Figs. S21 and S22, with the same excellent results. The above experiments demonstrates that CPDs can successfully detect Cu2+ and S2− in cells by the fluorescence “on–-off-on” process, which suggests the potential of CPDs for bioimaging and contributes a new approach for the treatment and diagnosis of diseases related to the above ions, such as PD.

In summary, CPDs was obtained by a simple and economical hydrothermal method, and its structure, composition and physicochemical properties were investigated in detail using a series of characterization tools, leading to the following conclusions: CPDs features unique structure, abundant functional groups, excellent photostability and outstanding biocompatibility. Based on these remarkable qualities, a smart sensing system was successfully prepared, which can determine the concentrations of Cu2+ and S2− in real time on-site by capturing the fluorescence color change on paper strip by color recognition software in the mobile phone. In addition, the utilization of CPDs as fluorescent ink for graphic encryption and anti-counterfeit printing is particularly valuable in the field of information security. Furthermore. There is also identification and adsorption of fluorescent hydrogel as well as imaging of living cell both acting on Cu2+ and S2− respectively, which are not only crucial for the industrial production and safety and security of environmental water quality, but also meaningful for biology and medicine that require ion level monitoring. Thus, this research project demonstrates the great potential of CPDs as an efficient optical sensor and provides precious experience for the detection of Cu2+ and S2− in future.
